session.user:
we need to complete the transition from plan to pr. there is no more plan noun. there are PRs that contain plans and are in a planned state.
first priority is the audit external apis. e.g. we are still returning "plan_id" out of json payloads. but we need to a thorough audit
session.assistant: I'll launch parallel Explore agents to audit the external API surface for plan terminology that needs to transition to PR terminology.

tool_use.name: Agent
tool_use.param: FIRST check docs/learned/index.md for existing documentation on this topic. Read relevant ...[truncated 911 chars]...rough - list every file and line where plan terminology appears in external-facing output.

FIRST check docs/learned/index.md for existing documentation on this topic. Read relevant ...[truncated 727 chars]....claude/commands/ - .claude/skills/
List every occurrence with file path and line number.

session.tool_result:
Perfect! Now I have enough information to compile a thorough audit. Let me compile the comprehensive report:
## Thorough Audit: Plan Terminology in External-Facing APIs
Based on my systematic search of the erk codebase, I've identified **extensive use of "plan" terminology in external-facing APIs** that should be updated to reflect the concept rename to "PR". Here's the complete audit:
### Summary
The codebase has **widespread plan terminology** across: - 40+ exec script JSON outputs - Status data structures - TUI data models - CLI command parameters - REST API-adjacent gateway code
---
### 1. JSON OUTPUT FIELDS IN EXEC SCRIPTS
#### Direct JSON field names that appear in external output:
**plan_number** (very common): - `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/plan_save.py:347` - JSON output - `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/setup_impl_from_pr.py:210-213` - `plan_number`, `plan_url`, `plan_title` - `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/close_prs.py:34,112` - TypedDict `plan_number` field - `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/add_plan_labels.py:34,112` - TypedDict `plan_number` field - `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/post_workflow_started_comment.py:36` - Example output - `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/add_plan_label.py:23` - CLI argument - `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/detect_plan_from_branch.py:12,20,53` - JSON output with `plan_number` - `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/create_pr_from_session.py:123` - JSON output
... [250 lines omitted] ...

Perfect! Now let me compile the complete audit report. I have gathered comprehensive information. Let me create a final summary organized by category:
## Audit Report: User-Facing CLI Text Referencing "plan"
Based on a comprehensive search of the erk codebase, I've identified all user-facing CLI text that still references "plan" when the concept has been renamed to "PR". Here's the complete inventory:
### **1. CLICK COMMAND DEFINITIONS (Command Names)**
The following commands have "plan" in their command names:
| File | Line | Command Name | |------|------|------| | src/erk/cli/commands/codespace/run/objective/plan_cmd.py | 10 | `@click.command("plan")` | | src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/add_plan_label.py | 22 | `@click.command(name="add-plan-label")` | | src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/add_plan_labels.py | 117 | `@click.command(name="add-plan-labels")` | | src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/create_impl_context_from_plan.py | 38 | `@click.command(name="create-impl-context-from-plan")` | | src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/detect_plan_from_branch.py | 58 | `@click.command(name="detect-plan-from-branch")` | | src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/extract_latest_plan.py | 24 | `@click.command(name="extract-latest-plan")` | | src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/get_plan_info.py | 27 | `@click.command(name="get-plan-info")` | | src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/get_plan_metadata.py | 43 | `@click.command(name="get-plan-metadata")` | | src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/get_plans_for_objective.py | 28 | `@click.command(name="get-plans-for-objective")` | | src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/get_pr_for_plan.py | 49 | `@click.command(name="get-pr-for-plan")` | | src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/objective_plan_setup.py | 131 | `@click.command(name="objective-plan-setup")` | | src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/plan_save.py | 487 | `@click.command(name="plan-save")` | | src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/plan_update.py | 44 | `@click.command(name="plan-update")` | | src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/register_one_shot_plan.py | 23 | `@click.command(name="register-one-shot-plan")` | | src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/update_plan_header.py | 97 | `@click.command(name="update-plan-header")` | | src/erk/cli/commands/exec/scripts/validate_plan_content.py | 84 | `@click.command(name="validate-plan-content")` | | src/erk/cli/commands/objective/plan_cmd.py | 522 | `@click.command("plan")` | | src/erk/cli/commands/pr/replan_cmd.py | 9 | `@click.command("replan")` |
... [128 lines omitted] ...
### **6. ERROR AND STATUS MESSAGES** Multiple error messages reference "plan" (this is a sample): | src/erk/cli/commands/objective/objective_save_to_issue.py | 179 | "Error: No plan found in ~/.claude/plans/" | | src/erk/cli/commands/implement.py | 169 | "Error: Plan #<number> not found" | | src/erk/cli/commands/branch/checkout_cmd.py | 478 | "Error: Cannot specify both BRANCH and --for-plan." | - **Widespread usage** of plan-related terminology throughout error messages, docstrings, and status output The terminology shift from "plan" to "PR" is pervasive, touching CLI command names, argument names, help text, docstrings, error messages, and documentation files.

Excellent! Now I have a comprehensive view. Let me create a final summary report by compiling all the findings:
## Comprehensive Audit Report: "Plan" Terminology in Data Models and External Formats
Based on my thorough audit of the erk codebase, here are ALL occurrences of "plan" terminology in data models, schemas, and external-facing interfaces that could potentially need to be renamed to "PR":
### 1. **Event Types (Serializable)**
**File:** `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/core/prompt_executor.py`
- **Line 67-70:** `@dataclass(frozen=True) class PlanNumberEvent:` - Event type with `number: int` field
This is part of the `ExecutorEvent` union type (line 102-114) that gets serialized/deserialized during Claude CLI streaming.
---
### 2. **Result Dataclasses (Serializable)**
**File:** `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/core/prompt_executor.py`
- **Line 141:** `plan_number: int | None` in `CommandResult` dataclass - This field gets populated from executor events and returned to callers
**File:** `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/plan_store/types.py`
- **Line 37:** `plan_identifier: str` in `Plan` dataclass - provider-specific ID (GitHub PR number as string) - **Line 75:** `plan_id: str` in `CreatePlanResult` dataclass - the created PR number
**File:** `/Users/schrockn/code/erk/packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/plan_store/create_plan_draft_pr.py`
... [124 lines omitted] ...

"Implement without saving" - Implement directly on the current branch without saving a plan PR. 3. "View/Edit the Plan" - Open plan in editor to review or modify before deciding.
⚠️ WARNING: Currently on 'master'. We strongly discourage implementing directly on the trunk branch. Consider saving the plan and implementing in a dedicated worktree instead.
If user chooses 'Create new branch and planned PR': 1. Run /erk:plan-save 2. Call ExitPlanMode to end the planning session.
If user chooses 'Implement without saving': 1. Create implement-now marker: erk exec marker create --session-id 04eac938-d634-46ff-ab9e-b451f0eedca7 \ exit-plan-mode-hook.implement-now 2. Call ExitPlanMode 3. After exiting plan mode, implement the changes directly on the current branch. Do NOT run 'erk exec setup-impl' or create a new branch.

Here's the audit plan:
---
# Audit: External API "plan" Terminology → "pr" Rename
**Rename mapping:** `plan_number` → `pr_number`, `plan_id` → `pr_id`, `plan_url` → `pr_url`, `plan_title` → `pr_title`
**Kept as-is:** `erk-plan` GitHub label and `[erk-plan]` title prefix.
## Summary Statistics
| Category | Count | |----------|-------| | JSON output fields | ~50 occurrences across ~25 files | | Data model fields | ~15 fields across ~11 files | | CLI arguments/options | ~14 parameters | | CLI command names | ~17 commands | | Help strings | ~44 strings | | Gateway ABC methods | 4 methods | | TUI provider interface | ~6 methods/fields | | Skill/command files | ~9 files | | **Total files affected** | **~53+ source files** |

1→import tomllib 2→from dataclasses import dataclass 3→from pathlib import Path 4→ 5→# Re-export LoadedConfig from erk_shared for backwards compatibility 6→from erk_shared.context.types import LoadedConfig as LoadedConfig 7→ 8→ 9→@dataclass(frozen=True) 10→class ProjectConfig: 11→ """In-memory representation of `.erk/project.toml`. 12→ 13→ Example project.toml: 14→ # Optional: custom name (defaults to directory name) 15→ # name = "dagster-open-platform" 16→ 17→ [env] 18→ # Project-specific env vars (merged with repo-level) 19→ DAGSTER_HOME = "{project_root}" 20→ 21→ [post_create] 22→ # Runs AFTER repo-level commands, FROM project directory 23→ shell = "bash" 24→ commands = [ 25→ "source .venv/bin/activate", 26→ ] 27→ """ 28→ 29→ name: str | None # Custom project name (None = use directory name) 30→ env: dict[str, str]
... [362 lines omitted] ...
session.assistant:
The `[plans]` config section has a single key: `repo` — it specifies an alternate GitHub repo to store plans in (e.g., a separate private plans repo). The TOML looks like:
```toml [plans] repo = "owner/plans-repo" ```
This maps to `plans_repo` on `LoadedConfig`.

tool_use.name: AskUserQuestion
tool_use.param: [{'question': 'The `[plans]` config section has one key: `repo` — specifies an alternate G...[truncated 262 chars]... with the terminology shift — PRs are the top-level concept now'}], 'multiSelect': False}]

Should this rename?"="we should have a github config seciton " user notes: we should have a github config seciton. You can now continue with the user's answers in mind.

1→"""Domain service ABC for plan operations (no TUI type dependencies).""" 2→ 3→from abc import ABC, abstractmethod 4→from pathlib import Path 5→ 6→from erk_shared.gateway.browser.abc import BrowserLauncher 7→from erk_shared.gateway.clipboard.abc import Clipboard 8→from erk_shared.gateway.github.types import PRCheckRun, PRReviewThread 9→ 10→ 11→class PlanService(ABC): 12→ """Abstract base class for plan domain operations. 13→ 14→ Contains operations that are independent of TUI display concerns: 15→ closing plans, dispatching, fetching content, and GitHub PR data. 16→ No TUI type imports (PlanRowData, FetchTimings, PlanFilters, BranchActivity). 17→ """ 18→ 19→ @property 20→ @abstractmethod 21→ def repo_root(self) -> Path: 22→ """Get the repository root path.""" 23→ ... 24→ 25→ @property 26→ @abstractmethod 27→ def clipboard(self) -> Clipboard: 28→ """Get the clipboard interface for copy operations.""" 29→ ... 30→
... [109 lines omitted] ...
124→ """Fetch CI failure summaries for a pull request.

1→"""Context types for erk and erk-kits. 2→ 3→This module provides the core data types used by ErkContext: 4→- RepoContext: Repository discovery result 5→- NoRepoSentinel: Sentinel for when not in a repository 6→- GlobalConfig: Global erk configuration 7→- LoadedConfig: Repository-level configuration 8→- InteractiveAgentConfig: Configuration for interactive agent launches (Claude or Codex) 9→- AgentBackend: Type for agent backend selection 10→- PermissionMode: Generic permission mode for both Claude and Codex 11→""" 12→ 13→from __future__ import annotations 14→ 15→from dataclasses import dataclass 16→from functools import cache 17→from pathlib import Path 18→from typing import Literal, Self 19→ 20→from erk_shared.gateway.github.types import GitHubRepoId 21→ 22→# Agent backend types 23→AgentBackend = Literal["claude", "codex"] 24→ 25→# Claude CLI permission modes: 26→# - "default": Default mode with permission prompts 27→# - "acceptEdits": Accept edits without prompts (--permission-mode acceptEdits) 28→# - "plan": Plan mode for exploration and planning (--permission-mode plan) 29→# - "bypassPermissions": Bypass all permissions (--permission-mode bypassPermissions) 30→ClaudePermissionMode = Literal["default", "acceptEdits", "plan", "bypassPermissions"]

session.assistant:
Here's the complete plan:
## Plan: Complete "plan" → "pr" Terminology Rename
**Rename mapping:** `plan_number` → `pr_number`, `plan_id` → `pr_number`, `plan_url` → `pr_url`, `plan_title` → `pr_title`
**Kept as-is:** `erk-plan` label, `[erk-plan]` title prefix, CLI command names (`plan-save` etc.)
**Config:** `[plans] repo` → `[github] repo`
**12 phases, bottom-up by dependency:** 1. erk-shared foundation types (gateway ABC, plan store, prompt executor, metadata schemas) 2. Config parsing (`[plans]` → `[github]` with backwards compat) 3. Status models (`PlanStatus` → `PrStatus`) 4. Core output filter 5. TUI data layer (`PlanRowData` → `PrRowData`) 6. Exec script JSON outputs (~25 scripts) 7. CLI argument/option renames (~14 params) 8. Help strings & error messages (~44 strings) 9. Top-level CLI commands 10. Gateway implementations 11. Tests 12. Skills/commands in .claude/
~53+ source files affected. Mechanical rename using `rename-swarm` or `libcst-refactor`.

tool_use.param: [{'question': '📋 Plan: Complete "plan" → "pr" Terminology Rename\nCurrent context: (wt:erk...[truncated 399 chars]...ion': 'Open plan in editor to review or modify before deciding.'}], 'multiSelect': False}]

session.tool_result:
User has answered your questions: "📋 Plan: Complete "plan" → "pr" Terminology Rename Current context: (wt:erk) (br:master)
What would you like to do with this plan?"="let's create an objective instead" user notes: let's create an objective instead. You can now continue with the user's answers in mind.
session.user:
# /erk:objective-create
Create a new objective through an interactive, guided process. You describe what you want to accomplish, and Claude proposes a structured objective for your approval.
## Usage
```bash /erk:objective-create ```
---
## Agent Instructions
### Step 1: Prompt for Description
Ask the user to describe what they want to accomplish:
``` What do you want to accomplish? Describe it however makes sense to you - goals, constraints, design decisions you've already made, context about the codebase, etc.
I'll structure it into a formal objective for your review. ```
Wait for the user's response. They may provide:
- A brief summary - A detailed brain dump - Existing notes or documentation - A combination of the above
### Step 2: Analyze and Explore
Based on the user's description:
1. **Identify key elements:** - What is the transformation/goal? - What design decisions are mentioned or implied? - What constraints exist? - What's the scope?
2. **Explore the codebase if needed:** - If the user references specific code, read it to understand context - If architecture decisions are mentioned, verify current state - Gather enough context to propose realistic phases
3. **Capture context for downstream implementers:**
During objective creation, you gather valuable context that would otherwise be lost: web searches, codebase exploration, user feedback, and your reasoning. **This context belongs in the objective issue.**
Ask yourself: "What would a future agent (or human) implementing this need to know?"
Examples of valuable context: - **Inventories**: Grep results showing all instances of a pattern, categorized by type - **Root cause analysis**: Why the current state exists, what caused the problem - **Codebase findings**: Relevant files, existing patterns, architectural constraints - **External research**: API docs, library behavior, migration guides discovered via web search - **Design rationale**: Why certain approaches were chosen or rejected - **User preferences**: Feedback and decisions made during the conversation
This context goes in a dedicated section after the Roadmap (see template below)
### Step 3: Ask for Structure Preference
After understanding the user's goals, present structure options using AskUserQuestion:
``` How would you like the objective structured? ```
**Options:**
1. **Steelthread (Recommended)** - Phase 1 is a minimal vertical slice proving the concept works end-to-end, followed by Phase 2 to complete the feature. Best when you can demonstrate value with a partial implementation. 2. **Linear** - Sequential phases, each building on the previous. Good when infrastructure must exist before any use case works (e.g., database schema before queries). 3. **Single** - One phase, one PR. For small, focused objectives that don't need phasing. 4. **Custom** - User describes their preferred structure
**Selection guidelines:**
- **Steelthread**: Default choice. Use when feasible - it de-risks the approach early by proving the concept works. - **Linear**: Use when a steelthread doesn't make sense - e.g., building a new abstraction layer that has no value until complete. - **Single**: Use for objectives small enough to implement in one PR (typically 1-2 days of work). - **Custom**: Use when the user has a specific structure in mind.
### Step 4: Propose Structured Objective
Write a structured objective proposal and show it to the user. Use the appropriate template based on their structure choice:
**Generating the Roadmap Section**
Do NOT manually write roadmap tables or metadata blocks. Instead, construct a JSON object describing the phases and steps, then generate the complete roadmap section:
```bash echo '<json>' | erk exec objective-render-roadmap ```
This outputs a complete `## Roadmap` section with phase headers, tables, test sections, and a machine-readable metadata block (auto-generated, guaranteed consistent with tables). Include the command's output directly in the objective body.
**JSON input format:** Each phase needs `name` (string) and `steps` (list of `{"id": "...", "description": "..."}`). Optional fields: `description` (phase description), `pr_count` (defaults to "1 PR"), `test` (acceptance test description). Each step can optionally include `"slug": "kebab-case-slug"` — if omitted, a slug is auto-generated from the description.
#### Steelthread Template (Recommended)
The objective body follows this structure. The `## Roadmap` section is generated by `erk exec objective-render-roadmap`.
```markdown # Objective: [Clear, Concise Title]
[1-2 sentence summary of the transformation]
## Goal
[What the end state looks like - concrete examples of the new API/behavior/architecture]
## Design Decisions
- **[Decision 1]**: [rationale] - **[Decision 2]**: [rationale]
## Roadmap
[INSERT OUTPUT from: echo '<json>' | erk exec objective-render-roadmap]
## Implementation Context
[Current architecture, target architecture, patterns to follow - include if helpful]
## Exploration Notes
[Context gathered during objective creation that implementers will need]
## Related Documentation
- Skills to load: [relevant skills] - Docs to reference: [relevant docs] ```
**Steelthread JSON example:**
```json { "phases": [ { "name": "[Name] Steelthread", "description": "Minimal vertical slice proving the concept works end-to-end.", "steps": [ { "id": "1.1", "description": "[Minimal infrastructure]" }, { "id": "1.2", "description": "[Wire into one command/path]" } ], "test": "[End-to-end acceptance test for steelthread]" }, { "name": "Complete [Name]", "description": "Fill out remaining functionality.", "steps": [ { "id": "2.1", "description": "[Extend to remaining commands]" }, { "id": "2.2", "description": "[Full test coverage]" } ], "test": "[Full acceptance criteria]" } ] } ```
#### Linear Template
Same body structure as Steelthread. Use this JSON pattern for the roadmap:
```json { "phases": [ { "name": "[Foundation]", "description": "[Infrastructure needed by subsequent phases]", "steps": [{ "id": "1.1", "description": "..." }], "test": "[Verification criteria]" }, { "name": "[Build on Foundation]", "description": "[Uses Phase 1 infrastructure]", "steps": [{ "id": "2.1", "description": "..." }], "test": "[Verification criteria]" } ] } ```
#### Single Template
Same body structure with one phase:
```json { "phases": [ { "name": "[Implementation]", "steps": [ { "id": "1", "description": "..." }, { "id": "2", "description": "..." } ], "test": "[Acceptance criteria]" } ] } ```
**Key structuring principles:**
1. **One PR per phase**: Each phase should be a self-contained PR that: - Has a coherent, reviewable scope - Includes its own tests (unit + integration as appropriate) - Leaves the system in a working state when merged
2. **Always shippable**: After each merged PR, the system must remain functional. Never leave the codebase in a broken state between phases.
3. **Test per phase**: Every phase needs a **Test:** section describing acceptance criteria. This becomes the definition of "done" for that PR.
**Section guidelines:**
- **Goal**: Always include - this is the north star - **Design Decisions**: Include if there are meaningful choices already made - **Roadmap**: Include for bounded objectives - break into shippable phases (1 PR each typically) - **Principles/Guidelines**: Include for perpetual objectives instead of a roadmap - **Implementation Context**: Include for larger refactors where current/target state matters - **Exploration Notes**: Include when you gathered valuable context during creation (inventories, research, reasoning). This is often the most valuable section for implementers - **Related Documentation**: Include if specific skills or docs are relevant
**For perpetual objectives**, replace the Roadmap section with:
```markdown ## Principles
- [Guiding principle 1] - [Guiding principle 2] ```
After showing the proposal, ask:
``` Does this capture what you're thinking? I can adjust any section, add more detail to the roadmap, or restructure the phases. ```
### Step 5: Iterate Until Approved
The user may:
- **Approve as-is**: Proceed to Step 6 - **Request changes**: Make the requested adjustments and show again - **Add information**: Incorporate new details and show again - **Restructure**: Reorganize phases/nodes based on feedback
Continue iterating until the user approves.
### Step 6: Write to Plan File and Create Objective
Once approved:
1. **Write the objective to scratch storage:**
```bash mkdir -p .erk/scratch/sessions/04eac938-d634-46ff-ab9e-b451f0eedca7/ ```
Write the approved objective content to:
``` .erk/scratch/sessions/04eac938-d634-46ff-ab9e-b451f0eedca7/objective-body.md ```
This is the Priority 1 lookup path for `objective-save-to-issue --session-id`.
2. **Generate a slug** from the objective content: - Create a concise, descriptive slug (3-5 words, kebab-case) that captures the essence of the objective - **Pattern:** `^[a-z][a-z0-9]*(-[a-z0-9]+)*$` - **Length:** 3-40 characters - **Rules:** lowercase only, must start with a letter, hyphens between words only, no consecutive hyphens, no underscores/spaces/dots - **Valid:** `build-auth-system`, `refactor-gateway`, `add-dark-mode`, `fix-tui-layout` - **Invalid:** `Build-Auth` (uppercase), `123-start` (starts with digit), `my--slug` (consecutive hyphens), `ab` (too short) - If the backend rejects the slug, fix it according to the rules above and retry
3. **Create the objective with validation:**
```bash erk exec objective-save-to-issue --session-id=<session-id> --slug=<generated-slug> --format=display --validate ```
This creates the objective and validates the roadmap in one step. If validation fails, fix the objective content and re-run.
4. **Report success:**
``` Objective #<number> created URL: <issue-url> Roadmap validation: clean (or "none (objective has no roadmap)" if no roadmap)
Next steps: - Use /local:objective-view <number> to view the dependency graph - Use /erk:objective-plan <number> to create plans for specific nodes - Track progress by updating node status in the issue ```
---
## Output Format
- **Start**: Single prompt asking what they want to accomplish - **After description**: Ask for structure preference - **After structure choice**: Show proposed structured objective - **Iteration**: Show updated objective after each change - **Success**: Issue number, URL, and next steps
---
## Differences from /erk:plan-save
| Feature | /erk:plan-save | /erk:objective-create | | ---------------- | ------------------- | ------------------------------- | | Label | `erk-plan` | `erk-objective` only | | Purpose | Implementation plan | Roadmap or perpetual focus area | | Title prefix | `[erk-plan]` | None | | Metadata block | Yes | No | | Commands section | Yes | No | | Body content | Metadata only | Objective directly | | Input | Existing plan file | Interactive creation |
---
## Types of Objectives
**Bounded objectives** - Have a clear end state and roadmap:
- "Refactor GitHub gateway into facade pattern" - "Add dark mode support"
**Perpetual objectives** - Ongoing areas of focus without a defined end:
- "Improve test coverage" - "Documentation maintenance" - "Performance optimization"
The structure adapts to the type - perpetual objectives may have principles/guidelines instead of a phased roadmap.
---
## Error Cases
| Scenario | Action | | ---------------------------- | -------------------------------- | | User provides no description | Re-prompt with examples | | Not authenticated | Report GitHub auth error | | Issue creation fails | Report API error, offer to retry | | Plan file write fails | Report error with path |
session.assistant: The plan we just created has all the context needed for this objective. Let me structure it.

tool_use.name: AskUserQuestion
tool_use.param: [{'question': 'How would you like the objective structured?', 'header': 'Structure', 'opti...[truncated 337 chars]...'description': 'One massive PR that renames everything at once.'}], 'multiSelect': False}]

session.tool_result: User has answered your questions: "How would you like the objective structured?"="Linear (Recommended)". You can now continue with the user's answers in mind.
session.assistant: Now let me construct the objective. I need to render the roadmap first.

tool_use.name: Bash

session.tool_result:
## Roadmap
### Phase 1: Foundation: erk-shared types and gateway ABCs (1 PR)
Rename all plan terminology in the shared package that everything depends on.
| Node | Description | Status | PR | |------|-------------|--------|----| | 1.1 | Rename gateway ABC: PlanService → PrService, plan_service/ → pr_service/, method params (plan_id → pr_number, plan_url → pr_url) | pending | - | | 1.2 | Rename plan_store types: Plan.plan_identifier → pr_number, CreatePlanResult.plan_id → pr_number, CreatePlanDraftPRResult fields | pending | - | | 1.3 | Rename prompt executor types: PlanNumberEvent → PrNumberEvent, CommandResult.plan_number → pr_number | pending | - | | 1.4 | Rename output types: PlanNextSteps.plan_number → pr_number, format functions | pending | - | | 1.5 | Rename metadata schemas: plan_number → pr_number in all schema classes, add migration from plan_number | pending | - | | 1.6 | Rename LoadedConfig.plans_repo → github_repo | pending | - | | 1.7 | Update all gateway implementations to match new ABC signatures | pending | - | | 1.8 | Update all direct importers/consumers of renamed types | pending | - |
**Test:** All imports resolve. Type checker passes. Existing tests pass after updating assertions.
### Phase 2: CLI layer: JSON output, arguments, and help text (1 PR)
Rename all user-facing plan terminology in CLI commands, exec scripts, and output formatting.
| Node | Description | Status | PR | |------|-------------|--------|----| | 2.1 | Rename JSON output fields in ~25 exec scripts: plan_number → pr_number, plan_id → pr_number, plan_url → pr_url, plan_title → pr_title | pending | - | | 2.2 | Rename TypedDict fields: PlanCloseItem.plan_number, PlanLabelItem.plan_number, SignalSuccess.plan_number | pending | - | | 2.3 | Rename Click arguments/options: plan_number → pr_number, plan_id → pr_number across ~14 commands | pending | - | | 2.4 | Update ~44 help strings and error messages referencing plan as a noun | pending | - | | 2.5 | Update output_filter.py and output.py: plan_number dict keys → pr_number | pending | - |
... [181 lines omitted] ...
<!-- WARNING: Machine-generated. Manual edits may break erk tooling. --> description: Update ~44 help strings and error messages referencing plan as a noun
session.assistant: Good, the roadmap rendered. Now let me write the full objective body and save it.
